slash_command_best_practices.overview: Best practices for creating effective, maintainable, and user-friendly slash commands in Roo Code.

principle.name: descriptive_names
principle.description: Command names should clearly communicate their purpose

guidelines.guideline: Use action-oriented verbs (review, generate, analyze, test)
Include the target or object (api-endpoint, readme, security)
Keep to 2-3 words maximum for brevity
Avoid abbreviations unless universally understood

rules.rule: Do not use underscores or spaces

automatic_processing.note: Roo Code automatically processes command names:
automatic_processing.processing: Replaces multiple consecutive hyphens with single dash

principle.name: avoid_conflicts
principle.description: Prevent naming conflicts with existing commands

guidelines.guideline: Check built-in commands before naming (they cannot be overridden)
Use project-specific prefixes for team commands
Document command purpose to prevent duplicate creations

structure.context: Provide immediate context about what to work on

examples.good: Create a REST API endpoint with the following specifications: - Proper error handling - Input validation - Authentication middleware
examples.bad: Please help me with an API endpoint if you can

principle.name: structured_lists
principle.description: Use lists for clarity and parseability

type.name: numbered
type.use: For sequential steps or ordered priorities
type.example: 1. Analyze current architecture 2. Identify bottlenecks 3. Propose improvements

type.name: bulleted
type.example: - Include error handling - Add input validation - Document all endpoints

guidelines.guideline: Mention project conventions when relevant
Reference existing patterns to follow
Specify coding standards or style guides
Include testing requirements

examples.example: Follow our project's REST API conventions and naming patterns
Use our established error handling middleware
Include tests following our Jest testing patterns

principle.name: specificity
principle.description: Be specific about expectations and requirements

prefer.specific: Identify O(n²) algorithms and suggest optimizations
Check for SQL injection vulnerabilities in query builders
Reduce memory usage by implementing lazy loading

field.name: description
field.purpose: Appears in command menu to help users choose commands

guidelines.guideline: Keep under 80 characters for readability
Start with action verb
Be specific about what the command does
Avoid redundancy with command name

examples.good: Generate a new REST API endpoint with best practices
Analyze code for security vulnerabilities and risks
examples.bad: This command generates tests for the selected code including unit tests and integration tests and more

field.name: argument-hint
field.purpose: Shows users what arguments to provide

guidelines.guideline: Use angle brackets: <argument-name>
Be specific and descriptive
Show multiple arguments clearly: <arg1> <arg2>
Use terms users will understand

field.name: mode
field.purpose: Switches to specific mode before running command

when_to_use.case: Command creates or modifies code files

guidelines.guideline: Use when command needs specific mode capabilities
Omit if command works in any mode
Ensure mode slug is valid and exists

principle.name: command_scope
principle.description: Choose appropriate scope for each command

use_project_scope.when: Command is specific to project structure

use_global_scope.when: Command is a personal workflow preference
User doesn't want to share with team

strategies.strategy: Use consistent prefixes for related commands
Group by functional area (api-, test-, docs-)
Use subdirectories for large command sets

principle.name: version_control
principle.description: Best practices for version controlling commands

project_commands.practice: Document commands in project README or docs
Keep commands updated with project evolution

global_commands.practice: Do not version control in projects
Share valuable commands by moving to project scope

checklist.item: Update commands when project conventions change
Refine prompts based on AI responses

testing_approach.step: Verify arguments are clear if using hints
Check mode switching works if specified

what_to_document.item: Purpose and use cases for each command
Examples of when to use the command

pitfall.name: overly_generic
pitfall.problem: Commands are too broad and don't provide enough direction
pitfall.example: Bad: "Review this code" Good: "Review this code for security vulnerabilities, focusing on authentication, input validation, and data sanitization"

pitfall.name: too_specific
pitfall.problem: Commands are so specific they're only useful once
pitfall.example: Bad: "Create endpoint for user ID 12345 with email validation" Good: "Create user management endpoint with validation"

pitfall.name: missing_context
pitfall.problem: Commands don't reference project conventions or patterns
pitfall.solution: Always include references to project-specific requirements

pitfall.name: poor_argument_design
pitfall.problem: Argument hints are unclear or missing when needed
pitfall.example: Bad: /create-component (no hint, but needs component name) Good: /create-component <component-name>

pitfall.name: duplicate_commands
pitfall.problem: Multiple commands do similar things with different names
pitfall.solution: Consolidate similar commands or differentiate clearly

pitfall.name: neglected_maintenance
pitfall.problem: Commands become outdated as project evolves
pitfall.solution: Review and update commands when conventions change

category.name: before_creation
category.item: Is the command name clear and descriptive?
Does a similar command already exist?
Is this the right scope (global vs project)?
Do I need arguments? If so, are they clear?

category.name: during_creation
category.item: Does the description clearly explain the purpose?

category.name: after_creation
category.item: Does the command appear in the / menu?
Is the description clear in the menu?
Does the command produce expected results?
Should this be documented for the team?
